hints.junior: The counter always says 1 because every click reads the same old 'count = 0' from when the handler was created. The fix is one word: 'prev'.
hints.mid: Stale closures in React almost always show up in useEffect with empty deps or in async callbacks. The exhaustive-deps lint rule is your early warning.
hints.staff: The bug is conceptually simple: a function scope is frozen at creation. The tricky part is recognising it across indirection layers in complex effect graphs.

hints.junior: Click the broken button 5 times fast. See? Always 1. Now try fixed: same fast clicking, correct count. That's the functional updater doing its job.
hints.mid: The broken version batches 5 calls of setCount(0 + 1). The fixed version queues setCount(prev => prev + 1) 5 times; each sees the result of the last.
hints.staff: React 18 automatic batching makes this worse in concurrent mode: multiple state updates in a batch all read the same stale closure value.

hints.junior: Install eslint-plugin-react-hooks if you don't have it. It will catch these before they hit production.
hints.mid: Think of useRef as a shared notebook everyone writes to; closures read from the notebook, not their private copy.
hints.staff: useEffectEvent is worth tracking in React 19: it's the principled resolution to the exhaustive-deps vs. stable-callback tension.

hints.junior: The concept chapter is the principle. Read it before the demo so the toggle makes sense.
hints.mid: Connect the principle to your component API: what prop or CSS change encodes the fix?
hints.staff: Map the pattern to observability: what would you measure in RUM or lab to prove the fix?

anecdotes.junior: I once debugged a form submission bug for hours: the async handler was sending the initial empty form data. Refs fixed it in one line.
anecdotes.mid: The pattern that finally made closures click for me: each React render is a snapshot. The handler carries that snapshot as a backpack forever.
anecdotes.staff: Teams I've worked with often suppress exhaustive-deps warnings wholesale. That's usually when closure bugs start accumulating silently in prod.

sections.junior: Hey! Today we're fixing a sneaky bug where your click counter always shows 1 even when you click 5 times. It's about closures, I promise it'll make sense.
sections.mid: This case is about stale closures in React hooks: why event handlers and intervals read old state, and three clean patterns to fix it.
sections.staff: We're covering the closure capture model, why React's exhaustive-deps rule exists, and the upcoming useEffectEvent primitive for stable callbacks.

sections.junior: The concept chapter is the principle. Read it before the demo so the toggle makes sense.
sections.mid: Connect the principle to your component API: what prop or CSS change encodes the fix?
sections.staff: Map the pattern to observability: what would you measure in RUM or lab to prove the fix?

sections.junior: Click the broken button 5 times fast. See? Always 1. Now try fixed: same fast clicking, correct count. That's the functional updater doing its job.
sections.mid: The broken version batches 5 calls of setCount(0 + 1). The fixed version queues setCount(prev => prev + 1) 5 times; each sees the result of the last.
sections.staff: React 18 automatic batching makes this worse in concurrent mode: multiple state updates in a batch all read the same stale closure value.
